title:
A company wants to implement defense-in-depth security for their web application.

**Which combination of services should be used?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: WAF + Shield + Security Groups + NACLs + KMS encryption.**

**Defense-in-depth** requires multiple security layers:

| Layer | Service | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Application | **WAF** | Application-layer filtering |
| Network edge | **Shield** | DDoS protection |
| Instance | **Security Groups** | Instance firewall |
| Subnet | **NACLs** | Subnet firewall |
| Data | **KMS** | Encryption |

A layered approach provides comprehensive security; a single layer is insufficient.

**Incorrect options:**

- Security Groups only
- IAM policies and Security Groups
- CloudFront with HTTPS only

**References:** Defense in Depth, Security Patterns

title:
A batch processing application needs to read messages from a queue, process them, and only delete messages after successful processing.

**Which service and pattern should be used?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: SQS with visibility timeout and explicit deletion.**

**SQS** supports a **visibility timeout** (it hides the message during processing); the message is only deleted after an explicit `DeleteMessage` call.

This ensures the message is not lost if processing fails - if processing fails, the message becomes visible again.

**Why the others are wrong**

- SNS doesn't persist messages
- Kinesis is for streaming
- EventBridge doesn't provide message persistence

**Incorrect options:**

- SNS with immediate deletion
- Kinesis with checkpointing
- EventBridge with Lambda

**References:** SQS, Message Processing Patterns

title:
A company wants to identify which EC2 instances are underutilized and could be downsized to reduce costs.

**Which AWS tool provides this recommendation?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Both B and C.**

**AWS Cost Explorer Right-Sizing Recommendations** is the primary tool for identifying underutilized EC2 instances to downsize, and **AWS Trusted Advisor** (via the Low Utilization EC2 Instances check) also flags underutilized instances.

Both analyze EC2 utilization metrics (e.g., CPU and network from CloudWatch) and recommend smaller instance types. (Memory is not collected by CloudWatch by default for EC2; it requires the CloudWatch Agent.)

**Why the others are wrong**

- **AWS Budgets** sets spending/alert thresholds but does not analyze instance utilization or recommend right-sizing.

**Incorrect options:**

- AWS Budgets

title:
An RDS database runs continuously but only needs Multi-AZ during business hours for high availability.

**How can costs be optimized?**

options.description: This is not possible; Multi-AZ cannot be toggled
options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Disable Multi-AZ outside business hours, re-enable during business hours.**

**Multi-AZ** can be enabled and disabled with no downtime (enabling may cause a brief increase in latency while the standby is provisioned). For non-production databases, you can disable it outside business hours to reduce costs.

- Disabling Multi-AZ removes the standby, roughly halving the instance-hour cost.
- The enable/disable cycle can be scripted with **Lambda**.

This approach is only suitable for **non-production workloads**.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **This is not possible; Multi-AZ cannot be toggled** — incorrect, it can be toggled.
- **Use Aurora Serverless** — does not directly solve the Multi-AZ cost issue.
- **Use read replicas instead of Multi-AZ** — read replicas serve a different purpose (read scaling), not HA failover.

*References: RDS Multi-AZ, Cost Optimization*

title:
A Lambda function is configured with 3008 MB memory but only uses 512 MB. The function runs 10 million times per month.

title:
Data transfer costs are high for a web application serving static content globally.

**How can data transfer costs be reduced?**

options.reasoning:

title:

**What architecture should be used?**

options.description: Fixed number of EC2 On-Demand instances
options.reasoning:

**Auto Scaling** automatically adjusts capacity based on demand, and **target tracking** scales based on a chosen metric.

- **Spot Instances** reduce costs (up to **90%**) for scale-out capacity.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Fixed number of EC2 On-Demand instances** — fixed capacity wastes money.
- **Large Reserved Instances** — Reserved capacity charges even when idle.
- **Manual scaling with CloudWatch alarms** — manual scaling is slow for instant spikes.

title:
An Auto Scaling group isn't responding to CloudWatch alarms despite the alarm firing.

**What's the most likely cause?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: The Auto Scaling group is in cooldown period.**

**Cooldown periods** suppress scaling actions to let metrics stabilize after a scaling event. While in cooldown, the ASG will not launch or terminate additional instances even if an alarm fires.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Wrong CloudWatch metric configured** — the alarm would not fire in that case.
- **Minimum size of ASG is 0** — would still allow scaling up from zero.
- **Desired size of ASG is 0** — would still respond to an alarm to increase capacity.

title:
You need to upgrade one EC2 instance in an ASG without receiving traffic.

**What should you do?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Put instance in Standby, upgrade, return to InService.**

Placing an instance in **Standby** detaches it from load balancing while keeping it in the ASG, which prevents it from being replaced during the upgrade.

After the upgrade, you return the instance to **InService**.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Hibernate the instance** — does not detach it from the load balancer.
- **Use cooldown timers** — unrelated to taking an instance out of service.
- **Use lifecycle hooks** — used for custom actions during launch/terminate, not for upgrades.

title: **How do you achieve WORM (Write Once Read Many) in S3?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Enable Object Lock at bucket creation.**

**Object Lock** enforces a WORM model using retention periods (and legal holds). It can be enabled at **bucket creation** time, or on an **existing bucket** via the console, CLI, SDK, or REST API (supported since November 2023).

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Enable versioning** — required as a prerequisite, but does not by itself enforce WORM.
- **Use S3 Glacier** — an archival storage class, not a WORM mechanism.
- **Configure bucket policy** — a policy cannot enforce immutable retention.

title: **What VPC feature provides private connectivity to S3 and DynamoDB without cost?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Gateway Endpoints.**

**Gateway endpoints** for **S3** and **DynamoDB** are free and route-table based. Traffic to these services stays within the AWS network instead of traversing the public internet.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **NAT Gateway** — incurs hourly and per-GB charges.
- **VPC Peering** — connects VPCs, not S3/DynamoDB.

title:
Internet-facing ALB requests fail despite healthy targets.

**What's likely wrong?**

options.description: ALB subnet route table lacks IGW route
options.reasoning:
**Correct option: ALB subnet route table lacks IGW route.**

An **internet-facing ALB** must reside in **public subnets** whose route tables include a `0.0.0.0/0` route to an **Internet Gateway (IGW)**. Without that route, inbound internet traffic cannot reach the ALB.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Targets in public subnet** — targets can be in private subnets; the ALB reaches them directly.
- **ALB has no Elastic IP** — an ALB does not require Elastic IPs.
- **Cross-zone LB disabled** — affects distribution, not basic reachability.

title: **What does a Multi-AZ RDS deployment provide?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Automatic failover for high availability.**

**Multi-AZ RDS** maintains a **synchronous standby** replica in a different Availability Zone to enable **automatic failover** for high availability. The standby is not used for reads.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Read scaling** — use read replicas instead.
- **Cross-region disaster recovery** — requires a cross-region read replica.
- **Cost optimization** — Multi-AZ increases cost (you pay for the standby).

title: **Which ECS networking mode provides task-level security groups?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: awsvpc.**

The **`awsvpc`** networking mode gives each task its own **ENI**, which enables **task-level security groups** and fine-grained network control.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Host** — tasks share the host's ENI and security groups.
- **Bridge** — Docker bridge networking; no per-task security group.
- **Default** — does not provide task-level security groups.

title: **How do you restrict S3 bucket access to specific IAM roles only?**

options.description: Bucket policy with explicit deny for all except listed roles
options.reasoning:
**Correct option: Bucket policy allowing only specific role; users assume role.**

Grant bucket access to a specific **IAM role**, then have users authenticate and **assume that role** before accessing the bucket. This centralizes access through role assumption.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Bucket policy with explicit deny for all except listed roles** — complex and error-prone to maintain.
- **Bucket policy with explicit deny for all users except listed** — grants by user, not by role assumption.
- **Bucket policy with NotPrincipal** — `NotPrincipal` is notoriously difficult to get right and is not the recommended pattern.

title:
CloudFront serves private content from S3.

**How do you grant access to multiple files?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: CloudFront signed cookies.**

**CloudFront signed cookies** grant access to multiple objects without requiring you to change the URLs. Once the cookie is set, the browser automatically sends it with each request to the protected paths.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **S3 pre-signed URLs** — operate at the S3 level and bypass CloudFront.
- **CloudFront signed URLs** — grant access to a single object each.
- **CloudFront geo restrictions** — control geographic access, not per-user authorization.

title: **Most cost-effective connectivity for small vendor team to VPC databases?**

options.reasoning:
**Correct option: AWS Client VPN.**

**AWS Client VPN** provides user-level authentication, **pay-as-you-go** pricing, and minimal setup — ideal for a small vendor team that needs occasional access to VPC databases.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **Direct Connect** — high fixed cost; overkill for a small team.
- **Site-to-Site VPN to VGW** — connects a whole site/network rather than individual users.
- **Site-to-Site VPN to Transit Gateway** — same site-level limitation, plus added TGW complexity.

title: **Serverless media processing pipeline with visual workflow and priority queues?**

options.description: S3 to Lambda to SQS to DynamoDB
options.reasoning:
**Correct option: S3 to Lambda to Step Functions to DynamoDB.**

**Step Functions** orchestrates the workflow and provides a **visual console** for monitoring. You can implement priority handling by routing work through separate entry points (state machine executions or task prioritization) into the processing stages.

**Why the others are wrong**

- **S3 to Lambda to SQS to DynamoDB** — SQS gives queuing but no visual orchestration workflow.
- **S3 to Lambda to SWF to DynamoDB** — SWF is a legacy service not recommended for new serverless designs.
- **S3 to Lambda to process to DynamoDB** — no orchestration or workflow visibility.
